paragraphs.text: The story from San Nicolas island must be taken with the same allowances as the reports of volcanic activity near the mouth of the Colorado river. Ignorant fishermen and sailors are easily alarmed by anything that has the appearance of the supernatural, and a heavy earthquake attended by changes in the familiar shore lines would be sufficient to spread fear. The island has never been held in favor with the Indians, which accounts for the fact that the lost woman of San Nicolas was allowed to live on the desolate spot for nearly a half century. She, by the way, did not belong to the original inhabitants whose skulls so thickly fill the soil of the island, but she had gone with her people from the neighboring mainland.
It was in transferring this handful of Indians to a reservation in Santa Barbara county that she was left on the island where she led a Crusoe life for so many years.
She forgot her native speech and adopted some unintelligible gibberish, which probably gave rise to the legend that she was the last descendant of the aborigines of the islands.
